algorithms.name: Bellman-Ford Algorithm
algorithms.analogy: Relax every road V-1 times — if prices still drop on the V-th pass, there's a price manipulation loop.
algorithms.story: Dijkstra breaks on negative weights — imagine a road where a city pays you $10 to cross. Bellman-Ford handles this by doing a simple 'relaxation' of every edge V-1 times. After V-1 iterations, all shortest paths are found (a path can have at most V-1 edges). A bonus: if running a V-th iteration still updates distances, a negative-weight cycle exists. Used in financial arbitrage detection and distance vector routing protocols (RIP).
algorithms.whenToUse:
• Graph has negative edge weights (Dijkstra fails)
• Need to detect negative-weight cycles
• Distributed shortest path (distance vector routing — RIP protocol)
• When graph is sparse and O(VE) is acceptable
• Financial arbitrage detection in currency exchange graphs
algorithms.howItWorks:
1. Initialise dist[] — dist[source] = 0, all others = infinity
2. Repeat V-1 times:
   For each edge (u, v, weight):
     If dist[u] + weight < dist[v]: dist[v] = dist[u] + weight (relax)
3. After V-1 iterations, dist[] holds shortest distances
4. Negative cycle check: run one more relaxation pass
   If any dist[v] updates: a negative cycle exists — return error
algorithms.interviewTips:
• Key differentiator from Dijkstra: handles negative weights, detects negative cycles
• Why V-1 iterations? A shortest path in a graph with V vertices has at most V-1 edges
• Time complexity O(VE) is slower than Dijkstra O((V+E) log V) for non-negative graphs
• In interviews: first ask if weights can be negative — if no, use Dijkstra
algorithms.useCases: [{"title":"Currency Arbitrage Detection","desc":"Model currencies as nodes, exchange rates as log-weight edges. Negative cycle = arbitrage opportunity — Bellman-Ford detects it."},{"title":"RIP Routing Protocol","desc":"Distance Vector Routing (RIP) is a distributed version of Bellman-Ford — each router exchanges distance estimates with neighbours."},{"title":"Network Delay Simulation","desc":"LeetCode 743: minimum time for signal to reach all nodes uses Bellman-Ford or Dijkstra depending on constraints."}]
algorithms.pitfalls: ["Not checking for Integer.MAX_VALUE overflow: dist[u] + weight overflows if dist[u] is MAX_VALUE","Forgetting the negative cycle check (V-th pass) — the problem might require detecting if no valid answer exists","Using Bellman-Ford when Dijkstra suffices — O(VE) vs O((V+E)logV) is a significant difference","Directed vs undirected: for undirected graphs, each undirected edge must be added as two directed edges"]
algorithms.variants: [{"name":"SPFA (Shortest Path Faster Algorithm)","desc":"Queue-based optimisation of Bellman-Ford — only relaxes edges from vertices whose distance improved. O(kE) average, O(VE) worst case"},{"name":"Yen's Algorithm","desc":"K shortest paths using Bellman-Ford as a subroutine"}]
algorithms.keyInsight: V-1 relaxations finds all shortest paths (a path has at most V-1 edges in a graph with V vertices). The V-th pass still updating = negative cycle exists. Guard Integer.MAX_VALUE overflow: always check dist[u] != MAX_VALUE before adding edge weight.
algorithms.patternSignal: negative edge weights, negative cycle detection, currency arbitrage, distance vector routing, shortest path with constraints, K stops limit
algorithms.practiceProblems: [{"name":"Cheapest Flights Within K Stops","difficulty":"Medium","hint":"Bellman-Ford with exactly K+1 relaxations. Use a copy of dist[] each iteration to prevent chain updates"},{"name":"Network Delay Time","difficulty":"Medium","hint":"Single-source shortest path — Bellman-Ford if you expect negative weights"},{"name":"Find Negative Cycle","difficulty":"Medium","hint":"Run V-th relaxation pass — if any distance updates, a negative cycle is reachable"},{"name":"Path with Maximum Probability","difficulty":"Medium","hint":"Negate log of probabilities to convert max product to min sum — then Bellman-Ford"}]

algorithms.name: Floyd-Warshall Algorithm
algorithms.analogy: For every pair of cities, ask: 'Is there a shorter route via city k?' — check every city as a potential waypoint.
algorithms.story: You have a distance matrix between all city pairs. Floyd-Warshall asks one elegant question V times: 'Would going through city k shorten the path from i to j?' — dist[i][j] = min(dist[i][j], dist[i][k] + dist[k][j]). After cycling through all k from 0 to V-1, you have the shortest path between every pair. The DP insight: the intermediate vertices allowed form a subset that grows by one vertex each outer iteration.
algorithms.whenToUse:
• All-pairs shortest path needed (not just from one source)
• Dense graphs where O(V³) ≈ O(V * (V+E) log V) from running Dijkstra from each vertex
• Graph is small (V <= 400 in competitive programming)
• Detecting negative cycles (diagonal becomes negative)
• Transitive closure (reachability matrix)
algorithms.howItWorks:
1. Initialise dist[i][j] = weight(i→j) if edge exists, 0 if i==j, infinity otherwise
2. For k from 0 to V-1 (try each vertex as intermediate waypoint):
   For i from 0 to V-1:
     For j from 0 to V-1:
       If dist[i][k] + dist[k][j] < dist[i][j]:
         dist[i][j] = dist[i][k] + dist[k][j]
3. After all k, dist[i][j] = shortest path from i to j
4. If any dist[i][i] < 0: negative cycle exists
algorithms.interviewTips:
• Three nested loops — the k loop is OUTERMOST (not innermost): classic mistake to get wrong
• Use INF = 1e9, NOT Integer.MAX_VALUE — adding two MAX_VALUE values overflows
• Can reconstruct the path by maintaining a 'next[i][j]' matrix
• Compare with Dijkstra run V times: O(V*(V+E)logV) — Floyd-Warshall is better when E is O(V²)
algorithms.useCases: [{"title":"Network Routing Tables","desc":"Compute shortest path between every router pair — used in link-state routing protocol (OSPF) for dense networks."},{"title":"Arbitrage Detection","desc":"Log-transform exchange rates, detect negative cycles with Floyd-Warshall — profitable currency loops."},{"title":"Transitive Closure","desc":"Which web pages can reach which other pages through links? Floyd-Warshall computes full reachability in O(V³)."}]
algorithms.pitfalls: ["K loop must be outermost: if k is the inner loop, intermediate vertices aren't fully explored before being used","Integer overflow with INF: always check dist[i][k] != INF && dist[k][j] != INF before adding","Confusing Floyd-Warshall (all-pairs) with Bellman-Ford (single-source) — different problems, different algorithms"]
algorithms.variants: [{"name":"Transitive Closure","desc":"Replace weights with booleans — reach[i][j] |= reach[i][k] && reach[k][j]"},{"name":"Minimax Path","desc":"Find path minimising the maximum edge weight: dp[i][j] = min(dp[i][j], max(dp[i][k], dp[k][j]))"}]
algorithms.keyInsight: K loop MUST be outermost — the most common implementation mistake. Use INF = 1e9, NEVER Integer.MAX_VALUE (overflow on addition). Negative cycle: if dist[i][i] < 0 after the algorithm. DP meaning: dist[i][j] after k iterations = shortest path using only vertices 0..k as intermediates.
algorithms.patternSignal: all-pairs shortest path, every pair of vertices, small dense graph, transitive closure, reachability matrix, negative cycle check all-pairs
algorithms.practiceProblems: [{"name":"Find the City With the Smallest Number of Neighbors at Threshold","difficulty":"Medium","hint":"Floyd-Warshall gives all-pairs distances, then count reachable cities per city"},{"name":"Evaluate Division","difficulty":"Medium","hint":"Floyd-Warshall on ratio graph: dist[i][j] = dist[i][k] * dist[k][j]"},{"name":"Number of Paths of Fixed Length","difficulty":"Medium","hint":"Matrix exponentiation is cleaner here, but Floyd-Warshall works for small V"}]

algorithms.name: Kruskal's MST Algorithm
algorithms.analogy: Always pick the cheapest available road that doesn't form a loop — until all cities are connected.
algorithms.story: You're building a highway network connecting all cities at minimum cost. Kruskal's strategy: sort all possible roads by cost, then greedily add the cheapest road as long as it doesn't create a cycle. Union-Find tells you instantly whether two cities are already connected. After V-1 edges are added, you have the minimum spanning tree — the cheapest network connecting all vertices. This greedy choice is provably optimal by the cut property.
algorithms.whenToUse:
• Find minimum cost to connect all nodes (MST)
• Sparse graphs where edge count E << V² (Kruskal's processes edges, not vertices)
• Network design: minimum cable, road, or pipe installation
• Cluster analysis by removing heaviest MST edges
algorithms.howItWorks:
1. Sort all edges by weight
2. Initialise Union-Find: each vertex is its own component
3. For each edge (u, v, weight) in sorted order:
   If find(u) != find(v): (adding this edge won't create a cycle)
     Add edge to MST
     union(u, v)
     If MST has V-1 edges, stop
4. Return MST edges (total weight = minimum spanning tree cost)
algorithms.interviewTips:
• Kruskal's uses Union-Find; Prim's uses a priority queue — know both and when to use each
• Kruskal's is better for sparse graphs (dominated by edge sort: O(E log E))
• Prim's is better for dense graphs (dominated by priority queue: O(V² or (V+E) log V))
• Union-Find with path compression + union by rank achieves nearly O(1) per operation (inverse Ackermann)
algorithms.useCases: [{"title":"Network Infrastructure","desc":"Minimum cost to lay fibre cables connecting all data centres — classic MST problem."},{"title":"Image Segmentation","desc":"Minimum spanning tree clustering: remove the K-1 heaviest MST edges to partition an image into K segments."},{"title":"Approximation Algorithms","desc":"MST-based 2-approximation for Travelling Salesman Problem — DFS traversal of MST gives a tour at most 2× optimal."}]
algorithms.pitfalls: ["Not using Union-Find — naive cycle detection with DFS is O(V) per edge, making total O(EV)","Union by rank is optional but prevents degenerate O(log V) per find without it","Disconnected graph: if MST has fewer than V-1 edges after processing all edges, graph is disconnected"]
algorithms.variants: [{"name":"Prim's Algorithm","desc":"Vertex-based MST: grow the MST greedily by always adding the cheapest edge crossing the cut. Better for dense graphs"},{"name":"Borůvka's Algorithm","desc":"Each component picks its cheapest outgoing edge simultaneously — O(E log V), parallelisable"}]
algorithms.keyInsight: Three steps: sort edges by weight, greedily add each if no cycle (union() returns true), stop at V-1 edges. If MST has fewer than V-1 edges after processing all edges, graph is disconnected. Kruskal's is better for SPARSE graphs; Prim's is better for DENSE graphs.
algorithms.patternSignal: minimum spanning tree, minimum cost to connect, Kruskal, union-find MST, sparse graph MST, cheapest network, connect all nodes
algorithms.practiceProblems: [{"name":"Min Cost to Connect All Points","difficulty":"Medium","hint":"Complete graph with Manhattan distance — Kruskal's on all n*(n-1)/2 edges or Prim's O(n²)"},{"name":"Connecting Cities With Minimum Cost","difficulty":"Medium","hint":"Standard Kruskal's MST — sort edges, union-find for cycle detection"},{"name":"Find Critical and Pseudo-Critical Edges in MST","difficulty":"Hard","hint":"For each edge: try excluding it (MST cost increases = critical), try forcing it (cost same = pseudo-critical)"}]

algorithms.name: Prim's MST Algorithm
algorithms.analogy: Grow a tree from a seed — always attach the cheapest available branch to what you've already built.
algorithms.story: Prim's algorithm grows the MST one vertex at a time. Start with any vertex. Repeatedly pick the cheapest edge that connects a vertex inside the growing tree to a vertex outside. A min-heap tracks the cheapest edge for each unvisited vertex. This is essentially Dijkstra's algorithm but tracking edge costs instead of path costs. Prim's is preferred for dense graphs because it avoids sorting all edges upfront.
algorithms.whenToUse:
• Find minimum cost to connect all nodes (MST) — same goal as Kruskal's
• Dense graphs (E close to V²) — Prim's heap-based approach beats Kruskal's edge sort
• Adjacency matrix representation (O(V²) Prim's is natural)
• When the graph is given as an adjacency list and memory is a constraint
algorithms.howItWorks:
1. Start with any vertex (typically vertex 0)
2. Add it to the MST set; add all its edges to the min-heap
3. While MST doesn't have all V vertices:
   a. Extract the minimum-weight edge from the heap: (weight, u, v)
   b. If v is already in MST: skip (would create a cycle)
   c. Add v to MST, record edge (u, v, weight)
   d. Add all edges from v to unvisited neighbours into the heap
4. Return MST edges when V vertices are included
algorithms.interviewTips:
• Prim's vs Kruskal's: same result, different approach — Prim's grows a tree, Kruskal's adds cheapest edges globally
• Lazy Prim's (above) allows stale entries in heap — check inMST[u] on extraction
• Eager Prim's (using decrease-key) is more memory-efficient but harder to code
• For V=100 dense graphs, use O(V²) Prim's with a simple array instead of heap
algorithms.useCases: [{"title":"Maze Generation","desc":"Generate random mazes by building a random spanning tree — Prim's with random edge weights produces natural-looking mazes."},{"title":"Cluster Connectivity","desc":"In distributed systems, build minimum-cost overlay network connecting all nodes in a cluster."},{"title":"VLSI Circuit Design","desc":"Minimum total wire length to connect all pins on a chip — direct MST application."}]
algorithms.pitfalls: ["Forgetting inMST check: lazy Prim's leaves stale entries in the priority queue — always check before processing","Undirected edges must be added in both directions in the adjacency list","Weight comparison: using int[] comparator by index 0 — ensure the weight is at index 0","Not initialising minEdge for source to 0 — source starts with edge cost 0"]
algorithms.variants: [{"name":"Eager Prim's","desc":"Uses decrease-key in a Fibonacci heap — O(E + V log V) total but complex to implement"},{"name":"Reverse Delete","desc":"Start with all edges, remove heaviest that don't disconnect the graph — theoretically O(E log E log E)"}]
algorithms.keyInsight: Prim's = Dijkstra but track minimum weight EDGE to include each vertex (not cumulative path distance). Always check inMST[u] on extraction — lazy Prim's leaves stale entries in the heap. Prefer Prim's over Kruskal's for DENSE graphs where edge count approaches V².
algorithms.patternSignal: minimum spanning tree, dense graph MST, Prim's algorithm, grow tree from seed, adjacency matrix MST, priority queue MST
algorithms.practiceProblems: [{"name":"Min Cost to Connect All Points","difficulty":"Medium","hint":"For dense complete graph, Prim's O(n²) without heap beats Kruskal's O(n² log n) edge sort"},{"name":"Network Delay Time","difficulty":"Medium","hint":"Dijkstra variant — Prim's is similar but tracks edge weight not path cost"},{"name":"Optimize Water Distribution in a Village","difficulty":"Hard","hint":"Add virtual node 0 connected to each house with well cost — then find MST"}]

algorithms.name: Cycle Detection in Graphs
algorithms.analogy: Walking through a city — if you reach a street you already walked today, you found a cycle.
algorithms.story: Detecting cycles is fundamental to graph algorithms: topological sort requires a DAG (no cycles), dependency resolution fails on circular dependencies, and deadlock detection finds cycles in resource graphs. The approach differs by graph type: undirected graphs use DFS tracking parent, directed graphs use DFS with a 'currently in stack' colour marker. Union-Find elegantly detects cycles in undirected graphs by checking if two vertices are already in the same component.
algorithms.whenToUse:
• Validate a graph is a DAG before topological sort
• Deadlock detection in OS resource allocation
• Dependency cycle detection (build systems, npm, Maven)
• Network loop detection
• Course schedule validation (can all courses be completed?)
algorithms.howItWorks:
DFS for Directed Graph (3-colour):
1. Colour each vertex: WHITE (unvisited), GRAY (in current DFS path), BLACK (done)
2. DFS from each unvisited vertex
3. When visiting a neighbour, if it's GRAY: back edge found → CYCLE
4. Mark vertex GRAY on enter, BLACK on exit

Union-Find for Undirected:
1. Initialise Union-Find with V components
2. For each edge (u, v): if find(u) == find(v): CYCLE
   Otherwise: union(u, v)
algorithms.interviewTips:
• Directed vs undirected is a critical distinction — ask upfront in the interview
• For directed graphs, GRAY = 'in current path' is the key insight — back edges create cycles
• For undirected, track parent to avoid treating the edge you came from as a cycle
• Course Schedule (LeetCode 207) = directed cycle detection → can all courses be completed?
algorithms.useCases: [{"title":"Course Schedule Validation","desc":"Can all courses be taken given prerequisites? Model as directed graph, return true iff no cycle exists."},{"title":"Deadlock Detection","desc":"Resource allocation graph: processes hold resources and wait for others — cycle = deadlock."},{"title":"Dependency Resolution","desc":"npm/Maven dependency resolution detects circular dependencies using directed cycle detection."}]
algorithms.pitfalls: ["Using visited[] alone for directed graphs: a visited node may not be on the current path — need GRAY to distinguish","Parent tracking for undirected: two-node cycles (A→B→A) are just one undirected edge — skip the parent","Disconnected graph: must start DFS from every unvisited vertex, not just vertex 0"]
algorithms.variants: [{"name":"Topological Sort","desc":"Only possible if no cycle exists — Kahn's algorithm (BFS) or DFS post-order"},{"name":"Floyd's Cycle Detection","desc":"For linked list cycles (not graphs): fast/slow pointer approach in O(n) time O(1) space"}]
algorithms.keyInsight: Directed vs undirected is critical — ask before coding. Directed: GRAY coloring (in current DFS path) catches back edges. Undirected: track parent to avoid treating the edge you came from as a cycle. Course Schedule = directed cycle detection. Union-Find elegantly handles undirected cycles without DFS.
algorithms.patternSignal: detect cycle directed, detect cycle undirected, course schedule prerequisites, deadlock detection, dependency cycle, topological sort prerequisite, circular reference
algorithms.practiceProblems: [{"name":"Course Schedule","difficulty":"Medium","hint":"Directed cycle detection — DFS with GRAY/BLACK coloring or Kahn's topological sort"},{"name":"Graph Valid Tree","difficulty":"Medium","hint":"Tree = connected + no cycle. Union-Find: n-1 edges and no cycle means it's a tree"},{"name":"Redundant Connection","difficulty":"Medium","hint":"Union-Find: the first edge where union() returns false (already same component) is redundant"},{"name":"Find Eventual Safe States","difficulty":"Medium","hint":"Safe = not part of a cycle and can't reach one. Reverse graph + topological sort or DFS coloring"}]
